column2: — Well, Australia.
— I'm not asking.
And as it is, you see, there's no shortage of worries.
— What kind?
— Lilichka got the idea of planting pumpkins — go out and look, they're already lying there.
Says they'll sell them to France in the autumn.
— Lord, — sighed Maria.
— Anything but these grand ideas.
We've had our fill of suffering from them, Mom.
— You know, the first night I cried, and then I thought that they aren't really like us after all, you understand?
They know what they're doing, they think completely differently.
— Maybe so, Mom.
— With the pumpkins, for instance, they want to make money, not do some public good for everyone.
And to take photographs.
— I know what you mean, Mom.
— So let them do it, don't put them off.
I believe in them, look at them — how young they are, each happy in her own way and unhappy in her own way.
— You know, I'm glad they realized you can live like this sooner than at fifty, — replied Maria, turning a bracelet of sandstone on her wrist.
— Could I ever have left the children for the summer like that with the girls' father?
Never.
But Lilichka, look, she can…
— You and I are different, dear.
I didn't know how to be with you the way I am with the girls.
Forgive me for learning to be like that only when you no longer needed it.
— I still need it, Mom.
— Well, I was a little better than my mother.
She couldn't forgive me these bushes and this cottage.
But she certainly would have forgiven, had she lived longer.
The grandmother remembered how her father used to come here to her.
He would stand and look at his daughter in embarrassment.
She, delicate and sweet, held Maria in her arms.
She was barefoot and stood amid the sand and gravel.
'Are you happy, dear?'
— her father asked.
And she, in reply, remembering her grated balcony, burst into tears.
Her mother came to her only for the last days of her life.
— Have you seen Lusya?
— asked the grandmother, driving away the memories.
— I have, of course I have.
Such an angelic little child.
— Ivan did come by after that, — said the grandmother.
— Lilya said they only spoke on the phone.
— Well, I'd just gone out to the gate, I wanted to find a pinecone for the little one, to hide some of those nuts in the cone for her, as if from a little squirrel…
— Mom, and you still have the energy for this, — Maria laughed.
— So there I am looking, a car pulls up.
A man got out of it.
— Get to the point, — said Maria impatiently.
— You just listen, — the grandmother retorted calmly.
— A man got out, in a suit.
Ivan.
— And what about him?
— Well, he started in on something, that he hadn't meant it that way, that he wants a better fate for the child, and I say to him: 'Ivan, did you come to take Lusya?'
— 'No,' he says.
'Then get in the car and drive back, and don't muddle the child's head.'
Maria, just don't tell the girls, because I didn't say anything.
— Mom, — said Maria, — she is his daughter, after all.
— I know that she's his daughter.
But the child has only just settled in here, has only just started sleeping at night, she's not a toy, she understands everything.
We told her that Daddy won't be around for now, so let that at least be the truth.
— So you've got a brand-new great-granddaughter, then?
— And you've got a granddaughter.
— Well, congratulations, Mommy.
— Congratulations to you, — retorted the grandmother.
Ustym
At night, when everyone was already asleep, a thunderstorm suddenly began.
It rained the way it only does in summer after a long drought.
Lightning flashed in the sky, and mad rolls of thunder followed it — as if someone were rolling empty steel barrels across the heavens.
The thunder crackled, and the lightning lit up the rooms of the house, which looked at the sky through its big windows; in the far bedroom Lilichka and Miyechka slept.
Lilichka slept soundly, the storm did not wake her.
Miyechka, on the other hand, flinched at the thunder, stared at the ceiling with wide-open eyes, she felt uneasy, as if she were the only soul in the whole house.

She watched the lightning light up her sister's bare leg, which had slipped out from under the blanket, watched the branches sway by the water, looked at her own skin, white in the bright light, raised her palm and waited for the lightning to blind her, followed by the thunder.
In another room Maria was trying to sleep.

From childhood she had loved sleeping during a storm in her parents' house, because it was the only place on earth where natural disasters did not threaten her, but in time, as the strength of her parents' arms kept weakening, and then it was no longer they who held her but Maria who held them, sleeping during a storm in her parents' house became scarier than anywhere else.
Her father's hands she had already let go of, but at her mother's she still clung.
Maria didn't want to take on the role of the eldest of them all, so she held on to her mother tightly.
Grandmother Teodora flinched at the thunder in the adjoining room.
Her window looked out onto two long-branched birches that menacingly waved their boughs, and she worried that they would fall on the house and kill someone.
For instance, the one who slept in the little room nearest the birches with its enormous windows — the newly minted great-granddaughter.
Beside her, her legs tucked under herself, sat pregnant Marta, holding the child by the hand.
The child's cobweb lashes quivered at the thunder, which seemed to be striking right on the roof above them.
Marta stroked her own two children with one hand, and with the other fingered Lusya's little fingers, one after another, one after another.
Lusya's room bordered, by its door, on Marta's, where she slept on a small sofa with her future children, and, by its wall, on the living room, where Maria slept.
Marta suddenly felt something drip onto her bare neck — she pinned up her hair for the night, because the strands would get tangled over the course of the night.
She shuddered.
It dripped once more and rolled in a cold drop down her spine.
Marta jumped to her feet, then Lusya began to cry.
Marta grabbed her — the little girl's side was already wet, and from the ceiling water was pouring in a thin little stream.
Outside the rain roared.
Marta, with the child in her arms, ran into the living room.
Maria was no longer asleep, because the sofa, which stood right up against the wall, had also begun to drip.
— We probably need to put something under it, — Maria whispered.
Lusya was crying.

The grandmother, frightened that her worst fears had come true and the birches had after all treacherously carried out an attack on those who least expected it of them, sprang up as far as her body, four years short of a century, allowed, and hurried to the living room, from which the child's crying came.
— Mom, — said Maria, — there's a leak somewhere.
The grandmother found the two of them, her and Marta, utterly helpless.
Miyechka sprang up at Lusya's crying, and then ran to the bathroom for a basin.
The water pattered down, booming against the bottom from above.
Only Lilichka slept.
— Mom, — Maria whispered to grandmother Teodora, walking her back to the bedroom, — doesn't it seem to you that this child isn't Lilichka's?
— We'll see, — said Teodora and lay down, listening into the darkness.
It seemed to her that the wind was still howling, but the thunder was receding, the lightning flashed dimly.
The storm had passed.
The morning was fresh and sunny.
What had happened in the night could only be told by the wet grass outside.
And in the house — by the basin of murky water that had collected from the ceiling over the night.
Only Lilichka had slept well — she had dreamed that she was running down a hill hand in hand with some unknown boy, and that she was fifteen again.
Lusya was finishing her sleep in Maria's arms, wrapped in a knitted little blanket that was too small for her, from under which two bare little feet peeked out.
— So then, — the grandmother began, — something has to be done about the roof.
As long as we've lived here, it's leaked, but usually it doesn't leak here.
So something has to be done.
After breakfast Marta and Lilichka went to the local artist, who also made some money on the side with ornamental drainpipes, weathervanes and roofs.
— Marta, listen, — said Lilichka on the way, — why don't you move into the room with Miyechka, and I'll sleep with Lusya.
Marta looked at Lilichka in silence.
— You need to get your sleep, — the latter went on.
— And so?
— said Marta.
— I don't want the child to disturb you.
Well, you understand that it's as if she were mine.
— She doesn't disturb me, Lilya, — retorted Marta.
The artist was hopelessly drunk and was itching to get up on the roof.
— I can fix it all for you right now if you want!
— he shouted, holding on to the fence of his yard.
Behind his back his wife stood and waved her hands, as if to say he wouldn't be able to.
A dog barked, hiding behind the woman.
— Do you have a ladder?
— shouted the artist, who, on top of everything, was also a bit deaf.
— We don't, — said Marta.
— I'll get up there anyway!
— he shouted.
— Is there a tree by the house?
— There isn't, — said Marta.
— Marta, — Lilichka whispered, — let's go now.
— Tell me when to come!
— the artist shouted after them.
— I feel sorry for him, — said Marta.
The midday heat was setting in, it was hard for Marta to walk under the sun.
— You need to get your sleep, — Lilichka began again.
— Lilya, — Marta stopped, — if you're being eaten up by guilt that everyone else is minding what's supposedly your child, then fight it.
This child has come to all of us, stop appropriating her.
Lilichka fell silent.
Marta walked on ahead.
Lilichka stood for a moment, then caught up with Marta and hugged her tightly from behind.
— So who do we call now?
— the grandmother fretted.
— What are we to do, sleep in water?
Let Lilya take the child and go into the city…
Marta glanced sharply at the grandmother and said:
— Granny, with money you can get anything done.
She was right — indeed, with money you could get anything done.
But not fix a roof in this place and at this time.
— Tomorrow's Ivan Kupala, after all, — said one craftsman.
— And so?
— asked Marta.
— You can't do any work.
— And if I add more money?
— My dear woman, I won't take the sin upon my soul.
— Well then, let's do it after Ivan Kupala, on Saturday.
— It's a long weekend, after all, I'm not climbing onto a roof after Ivan Kupala.
Do you want me to kill myself?
— Well, maybe you know someone who'd do the repair?
— Well, go and look for someone who'd take the sin upon his soul…
Marta walked around for an hour with the phone to her ear.
Until she was called to lunch.
She came up to the table and said:
— No one will take the sin upon his soul.
— What sin?
— asked Maria.
Lusya sat on her knee.
— The sin of working.
And the sin of earning money.
— Maybe offer more?
— asked the grandmother.
— I did, more than once.
I offered twice as much.
— And what, no one wants to?
— Miyechka spoke up.
— No one wants the money, — said Marta.
— If they go and earn on Ivan Kupala, then the Lord will punish them with destitution.
— And with alcoholism, — said Miyechka.
— Girls, — the grandmother spoke up, — go and look what the weather will be, whether we'll make it past Ivan Kupala, or whether we'll be sleeping in a puddle.
For the whole weekend the phones showed rain.
There was only the one sunny day left, tomorrow.
Miyechka got up from the table and said:
— I'll find a craftsman.
— God help you, — Maria laughed.
Miyechka took the phone and went out past the gate.
The birches rustled, the grass had already dried after the night's downpour.
Moisture was evaporating from the earth.
It was muggy and smelled of summer: wet burdock, nettles, flowers that had finished blooming.
She sat down on the grass, spreading her jacket under her so the ants wouldn't crawl on her legs, opened the messages and wrote:
